CASE MANAGEMENT CONFERENCE
Off Calendar Dec-26-2025 Set For Trial On Nov-09-26 At 09:30 A.M. In Dept 206

CASE MANAGEMENT CONFERENCE
Off Calendar Oct-17-2025 Continued To Jan-07-26 At 10:30 A.M. In Dept 610

DEMURRER to Amended COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Sep-29-2025 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

Defendant State of California's Demurrer to Amended Complaint is SUSTAINED WITH LEAVE TO AMEND.

Defendant State of California's Demurrer to Amended Complaint is SUSTAINED WITH LEAVE TO AMEND.

Defendant demurs under Code of Civil Procedure section 430.10(e) The issue under section 430.10(e) is, taking the facts properly pleaded and properly noticed as true, does the challenged cause of action necessarily fail to state a claim for relief. (Blank v. Kirwan (1985) 39 Cal.3d 311, 318.) In assessing whether the complaint states a cause of action, the court accepts all properly pleaded material facts, but not contentions, deductions, or conclusions of fact or law. (Minton v. Dignity Health (2019) 39 Cal.App.5th 1155, 1161.) The court liberally construes the complaint pursuant to Code of Civil Procedure section 452.

Defendant's demurrer to Plaintiff Karen Adriana's second cause of action-negligence is well taken. Plaintiffs first cause of action is alleged under general tort principles of negligence. Under the Government Claims Act, a tort action cannot be maintained against a government entity unless the claim is premised on a statute providing for that liability. (See Gov. Code 815.) No statutory basis for direct claims against a public entity for negligent exists. (Eastburn v. Regional Fire Protection Auth. (2003) 31 Cal.4th 1175, 1183.) In their opposition brief, Plaintiffs contend they can string together various provisions of the Act-sections 835, 840.2 and 815.2-to state a claim against Defendant. String cites-Plaintiffs' string refers to "inter alia" seven different statutes without any explanation-are not adequate. Plaintiffs must identify the statutory basis specifically and allege facts that support each element. Even if string cites were adequate, Plaintiffs have not alleged facts sufficient to state a claim under all their authorities. The allegations under section 840.2 are particularly lacking. Finally, even if corrected, the allegations may support a claim for dangerous condition of public property-already pled by Plaintiffs as the first cause of action-but not general negligence.

Defendant's demurrer to Plaintiff's first cause of action-dangerous condition of public property-is well taken, too. Again, Plaintiffs may have intended to allege a claim of dangerous condition of public property under sections 835, 840.2 and 815.2, but this is not how the complaint reads, nor are the allegations sufficient.

The court grants Plaintiffs leave to amend; any first amended complaint must be filed within 20 days of notice of this order.

Order is signed.

Judge: Joseph M. Quinn; Clerk: M. Macadangdang; Not Reported. (302/JMQ)
